user_input:
Dear HPC Team,
I am currently using the DAWN cluster and would like to leverage the Intel GPUs for running my PyTorch code.
According to the documentation, the following setup works well:
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
And in Python:
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex
...
1. Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')
model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
This setup functions as expected.
However, the pytorch-gpu environment appears to be a system-provided environment. For the purposes of my project, I would like to create and manage my own local Conda environment that is compatible with the Intel GPUs (i.e., includes PyTorch and the Intel Extension for PyTorch with xpu support).
I have attempted to clone the pytorch-gpu environment as well as install all of its modules manually, but I run into repeated HTTP 403 errors when trying to fetch packages from the Intel Conda channel. This seems to indicate restricted access or deprecation of some packages or channels.
Could you please advise on the recommended way to:
1.  Create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN,
2.  Ensure installation of all necessary packages without running into access issues?
If there is a supported method to export or replicate the pytorch-gpu environment into a user-managed Conda environment, that would be ideal.
Thank you very much for your assistance!
Best wishes,
Moritz
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Environment / Dawn PVC / PyTorch-IPEX
Technical Action Required: No (user setup guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User wants a modifiable local environment equivalent to centrally managed `pytorch-gpu`. Central env is immutable. Supported options:
- activate central `pytorch-gpu` then layer user virtualenv, or
- build user conda env with compatible pinned versions from Intel channel and test on PVC nodes.

ACTION
1) Confirm central env baseline works.
2) Explain central env cannot be modified.
3) Provide two supported approaches:
   - `pytorch-gpu` + user virtualenv layering
   - standalone conda env with compatible pins (`intel-extension-for-pytorch`, `pytorch`, python range) and Intel repo
4) Require testing on PVC/PVC9 interactive node.
5) Set required env vars for XPU runtime test.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Moritz,

`pytorch-gpu` is centrally managed and cannot be modified directly.

Supported options are:
1) Activate `pytorch-gpu`, then activate your own virtualenv for extra packages.
2) Create your own conda env with compatible versions (e.g. pinned `intel-extension-for-pytorch` + `pytorch`) and test it on PVC nodes.

Please use the Dawn guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html#machine-learning-data-science-frameworks

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not advise modifying centrally managed environments.

Technical Action Required: Yes [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN, and ensure the installation of all necessary packages without running into access issues [1]. The pytorch-gpu environment is a system-provided environment, and the user wants to create and manage their own local Conda environment [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To create a clone of the pytorch-gpu environment, you can use the following command: `conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1` [3]. Alternatively, you can create a new Conda environment from scratch and install the necessary packages, including PyTorch and the Intel Extension for PyTorch, using the following command: `conda install intel-extension-for-pytorch=2.3.110 pytorch=2.3.1 -c https://software.repos.intel.com/python/conda -c conda-forge` [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message you are encountering when trying to fetch packages from the Intel Conda channel?
- Have you tried loading the intelpython-conda module before creating your own Conda environment?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To create a local Conda environment compatible with the Intel GPU setup used on DAWN, you can try cloning the pytorch-gpu environment using the command: `conda create --yes --prefix "${FULL_VENV_NAME}" --clone pytorch-gpu-2.3.1` [3]. Alternatively, you can create a new Conda environment from scratch and install the necessary packages using the command: `conda install intel-extension-for-pytorch=2.3.110 pytorch=2.3.1 -c https://software.repos.intel.com/python/conda -c conda-forge` [4]. Please let me know if you encounter any issues.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when creating and managing your own Conda environments to avoid conflicts with system-provided environments [1].
- Make sure to use the correct channels and package versions to ensure compatibility with the Intel GPU setup on DAWN [1][3].

I am at a bit of a loss as to how access storage provided by AIRR/Dawn?
My storage name is airr-p12-storage
My user id is dn-stan1.
I have looked extensively in the support/help docs but must be looking straight past this information.
Thanks
Phill

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Path Discovery
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not locate AIRR/Dawn storage path in docs. The exact project path is user/project-specific and should be discovered from the user account rather than assumed.

ACTION
1) Ask user to check available RDS links under home:
   - `ls -ld ~/rds/*`
2) Ask user to identify the AIRR project path from quota/project listing:
   - `quota`
3) Confirm the matching path format once identified (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>` symlink).
4) Clarify that `~/rds/...` is a convenience link to the underlying project path.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Phil,

Your AIRR storage path is user/project specific. Please run:

`ls -ld ~/rds/*`
`quota`

This will show the project link available in your account (typically `~/rds/rds-<project-id>`).  
That entry is a symlink to the underlying project storage location.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not hardcode project-specific paths unless verified for that user/account.

user_input:
Hi -
I’ve just sbatched a job on the HPC, using cclake, job 9082481_[1-2]. If I squeue -u has27 it tells me (under NODELIST) 'ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance’. But maintenance finished last week.
Can you tell me what (if anything) I’ve done wrong? Script below.
Thanks,
Hamish
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A GLOVER-SL3-CPU
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --mail-type=NONE
#SBATCH -p cclake  # name of the partition to run job on
#SBATCH -o /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/bowtie2/bowtie2%a.log
#SBATCH -c 8         # number of CPUs. Default: 1
#SBATCH --mem=27360M     # RAM memory.
#SBATCH -t 12:00:00  # time for the job HH:MM:SS. Default: 1 min
#SBATCH -a 1-2
source ~/.bashrc
1. activate conda environment
conda activate bowtie2
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean
1. List all files in the folder starting with A or B,
2. then get the relevant line
INDIV_ID=$(ls -d [AB]* | head -n ${SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID} | tail -n 1)
cd $INDIV_ID
READ1=$(ls *1.fq.gz)
READ2=$(ls *2.fq.gz)
cd /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/Genomes/LSRagtag
bowtie2 -p 8 -x LSRagtag -1 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ1 -2 /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/soapnuke/clean/$INDIV_ID/$READ2 | samtools sort > /home/has27/rds/hpc-work/f2reads/align-LSRagtag/$INDIV_ID.sorted.bam
—
Dr Hamish Symington
Junior Research Fellow, Queens’ College, Cambridge
Academic Skills Tutor and Bye-Fellow, Downing College, Cambridge
Postdoctoral Researcher in the Glover Lab, Department of Plant Sciences, University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm Scheduling Status Interpretation
Technical Action Required: No (explanation + minor script advice)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User saw scheduler reason text and assumed there was a post-maintenance fault. From the script provided, there is no obvious submission syntax problem, and this kind of reason string can still appear while a job is simply pending under normal scheduler priority. Additional optimisation: explicit `--mem` is redundant for cclake in this case.

ACTION
1) Check live job state and reason (`squeue`, `scontrol`).
2) Explain pending-by-priority behavior.
3) Suggest removing redundant `--mem` directive when CPU request already implies desired memory.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Hamish,

From the script you sent, the job appears to have submitted correctly and may simply be pending under normal scheduler priority.

For this script, `--mem=27360M` is redundant if you already request the corresponding CPU count on `cclake`; removing it can simplify scheduling requests.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish temporary reason strings from real submission errors.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Scheduling / Node Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user's job is pending with a "ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance" status, despite the maintenance being scheduled to have finished last week [1][2]. This suggests that there may be an issue with the node or the scheduling system.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Check the current status of the cclake nodes and investigate any potential issues that may be causing the "ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance" status [1][2]. Consider checking the Slurm logs and node status to determine if there are any underlying issues [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide more information about the job you submitted, including the script and any error messages you received?
- Have you tried resubmitting the job or checking the status of the nodes using the `sinfo` command [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting this issue. We are investigating the cause of the "ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance" status and will update you as soon as possible. In the meantime, please try resubmitting your job or checking the status of the nodes using the `sinfo` command.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- It is possible that there may be an issue with the node or the scheduling system that is causing the "ReqNodeNotAvail, Reserved for maintenance" status [1][2].
- Users should be aware of any scheduled maintenance and plan their jobs accordingly [3].

[4] : HPCSSUP-45425

user_input:
Dear sir/madam,
I have been told to contact this email to ask for an MFA because I see the error: ‘When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.’.
Thank you very much for sorting.
Many thanks,
James
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Enrollment / Client Rendering Issue
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
Mac terminal rendering can distort SSH QR code during MFA enrollment. Enrollment can still proceed by scanning if possible, or manually entering the secret key in authenticator app.

ACTION
1) Confirm issue is known for some Mac terminal setups.
2) Provide manual secret-key fallback method.
3) Clarify expected token labels for SSH vs web.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

This is a known issue on some Mac setups where the QR may render poorly. You can still complete setup by manually entering the secret key in your authenticator app if scanning fails.

Please follow:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html

The SSH token should appear as `CSD3: SSH Login` (distinct from login-web token naming).

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not reset MFA unnecessarily when manual enrollment path is available.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA / SSH Connection
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with MFA when trying to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk via SSH, with the QR code being replaced by garbage or broken up by blank lines [1][2]. This is a known issue that may require a reset of the MFA secret [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, we can try resetting the MFA secret for the user. However, this will require a quick video call to verify the user's photo ID [4]. We will need to schedule a time for the call and send an MS Teams meeting invite.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please confirm your username and the exact error message you are seeing when trying to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk?
- Have you tried manually entering the secret key into an authenticator app [2]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
We can assist you with resetting your MFA secret. However, we will need to verify your photo ID over a video call. Please let us know a suitable time for the call, and we will send an MS Teams meeting invite.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- We must verify the user's photo ID before resetting the MFA secret [4].
- The user should be instructed to use the correct username when connecting to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk [4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54226
[2] : HPCSSUP-94321
[4] : HPCSSUP-73968

Hi Support Team,
I’m working on some SRCNet related projects and am attempting to run a containerised workflow on Azimuth which requires both CUDA and MPI support. I have built the required software built into an Ubuntu container with specific CUDA and MPI versions, but these of course need to be compatible with what is possible to deploy on the instance itself.
Is there a guide to how to set these up correctly? Or some experience with running GPU and MPI codes on Azimuth that I can leverage so that we can progress the SRC work?
I’m happy to provide the Dockerfile so that you can see how things are being built, and information about the code I’m trying to run, if useful.
Kind regards,
Bradley Meyers

Escalation Required: Optional (if capacity constrained)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User needed guidance for CUDA+MPI in Azimuth GPU VM. Recommended approach: install CUDA toolkit and Nvidia driver in VM, prefer Singularity for HPC container runtime with GPU bindings, and choose MPI runtime inside container as needed. Capacity caveat: GPU pool may be fully utilised.

ACTION
1) Confirm current GPU capacity availability.
2) Advise VM-level CUDA/driver install for GPU instances.
3) Recommend Singularity/Apptainer over Docker for HPC runtime.
4) Explain MPI runtime can be selected within container image.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bradley,

On Azimuth GPU VMs, you can install the CUDA toolkit and NVIDIA open-source driver in the usual way. For HPC workflows we generally recommend Singularity/Apptainer rather than Docker, including GPU bindings to expose host GPUs.

MPI runtime selection is up to your container build choice.

Please note GPU capacity may currently be fully allocated, so provisioning can be constrained.

Thanks,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply immediate GPU availability when capacity is saturated.

user_input:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope you are doing well.
I am writing to inquire about activating a Conda environment in Jupyter
Notebook within the Login-Web interface. I have already created a Conda
environment with ipykernel installed. However, when I start a Jupyter
Notebook session, I am unable to switch the kernel to my Conda environment.
Could you please provide guidance on how to properly activate and use my
Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook?
Thank you for your time and assistance.
Best regards,
Patrick
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter / Login-Web / User Environment
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User could not select a conda environment kernel in Login-Web Jupyter. Resolution is to ensure the environment has `ipykernel` and is explicitly registered as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen the notebook session.

ACTION
1) Confirm environment prerequisites
   - Environment exists and can be activated.
   - `ipykernel` is installed in that environment.
2) Register the environment as a Jupyter kernel
   - Activate env and run:
     `python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"`
3) Ask user to restart/reopen Login-Web Jupyter session and select the new kernel.
4) If kernel still does not appear, collect diagnostics:
   - `jupyter kernelspec list`
   - exact env activation/install commands used.
5) Share official docs for full workflow/reference.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patrick,

Please register your conda environment as a Jupyter kernel, then restart/reopen your Login-Web Jupyter session.

Example:

```bash
conda activate <env_name>
python -m ipykernel install --user --name <env_name> --display-name "Python (<env_name>)"
```

After that, select `Python (<env_name>)` from the Jupyter kernel menu.

Full guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#adding-virtual-environments

If it still doesn’t appear, please share:
- `jupyter kernelspec list`
- the exact commands you used to create and register the environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Keep instructions aligned with the documented Login-Web kernel registration workflow.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Jupyter Notebook / Conda Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
To activate a Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook within the Login-Web interface, the user needs to install the ipykernel into their Conda environment [1]. Then, they need to run `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` to make the Conda environment available as a kernel in Jupyter Notebook [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Install ipykernel in the Conda environment using `pip install ipykernel` [1].
2. Run `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` to make the Conda environment available as a kernel in Jupyter Notebook [1].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To use your Conda environment in Jupyter Notebook, you need to install the ipykernel and then make it available as a kernel. You can do this by running `pip install ipykernel` and then `ipython kernel install --user --name=[conda_venv_name]` [1]. If you have any issues, please let us know.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The user should follow the instructions provided in the documentation to avoid any potential issues [1][2].
- The Login-Web interface does not allow modification of environments within Jupyter Notebook [2].

Dear whom it may concern,
project id: pnk0p5ltryy
I hope this email finds you well. I am using RStudio as directed through RDS Farm. However, when I try to install packages(basic ones like dplyr and tidyverse),error occurs saying "error reading from connection". I tried to use web to access CRAN, but it seems I don't have internet connection on the virtual workspace. I would really appreciate your guidance, thank you very much.
Sincerely,
Yira
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Management / Restricted Network
Technical Action Required: Yes (staff-managed package availability)
Escalation Required: Yes (SRCP team)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on SRCP RStudio could not install CRAN packages due to no external internet by design. SRCP policy requires curated package availability managed by staff; users may need to use internal repo mirror and request missing packages.

ACTION
1) Explain SRCP security model (no direct external repo access).
2) Escalate to SRCP team for package provisioning.
3) Ask user for package list.
4) Provide internal repo usage (`options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")`).
5) Track/fork to dedicated package request ticket if needed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Yira,

On SRCP, direct access to external package repositories is restricted by design. Please use the internal R repo:

options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")
available.packages()

If required packages are still missing, please send the exact package list and we can add them through the SRCP process.

Regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not suggest bypassing SRCP network restrictions.

The user is experiencing issues installing R packages (e.g., dplyr, tidyverse) in RStudio via RDS Farm, with an error message indicating "error reading from connection" [1]. This suggests a potential issue with accessing the package repository or internet connectivity within the RDS Farm environment [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should be directed to use the internal SRCP repository for package installation, as internet access is blocked on SRCP [1]. The user can set the repository using the command `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` and then attempt to install the required packages [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm that you have set the internal repository as described above and attempted to install the packages again?
- Are there any specific error messages or issues that occur when trying to install packages using the internal repository?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To install R packages, please use the internal SRCP repository by setting the options as follows: `options(repos = "http://srcp-r-repo/")` [1]. Then, attempt to install the required packages. If you encounter any issues or error messages, please reply with the details.
Best regards,
HPC Support

- Ensure that users are aware of the internal repository and its usage to avoid issues with package installation [1].
- Be cautious of potential security risks associated with internet access within the RDS Farm environment [1].

Dear HPC support,
I am a PhD student with Pietro Lio project cs181 which has access to the Ampere node partition.
I have a need for some CPU only runs on the icelake nodes and was wondering if there's anyway to get this?
Many thanks
James Rowbottom - jr908
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 December 2024 17:25
To: James Rowbottom <jr908@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: New Tier2 Account on CSD3 (jr908)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Before you login for the first time, please use your SSH
public key to login via SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
Once MFA is configured, please use your SSH public key to
login via SSH to
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge
Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your
project's scratch directory (rds-t2-cs181). Please note that
this is shared per project so each user will probably wish to create
their own subdirectory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A t2-cs181-XXX
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A t2-cs181-XXX ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for DiRAC jobs are currently:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7
Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you
to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development
work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519

SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
Cascade Lake uses a RHEL7-compatible O/S
Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7
Cascade Lake login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Cascade Lake cluster. login-icelake will take you
to a different set of RHEL8 login nodes best suited for development
work for Ice Lake, Sapphire Rapids and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake*, sapphire* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Csh2001%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C45f1802bed5f4794f55108dc54b19708%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638478368922986884%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=CWOdOMH0UPXG%2FbLcyKonQ8u7LWG%2Bi%2BDUA6zg0BQ6u4M%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3420MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk

spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc

------------

448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job

Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (pmwd2)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3) facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8, where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on that type) you may alternatively SSH to login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes, but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system but present additional development packages tuned to the node hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module (hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack see [http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html).)
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Hi ,
I am writing to seek your assistance with logging into the Dirac machine. I encountered a similar issue before.
I have completed all the initial setup steps and obtained the QR code from the web portal token some time ago. However, it has been a while since I last logged into the machine. While I can still access the safe account, I am unable to log in to the web portal( ( Link<[https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s](https://id.hpc.cam.ac.uk/auth/realms/CSD3/protocol/openid-connect/auth?response_type=code&scope=openid&client_id=login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk&state=D0OAg-WHtN1N7fTXG-9dpjl1oOY&redirect_uri=https%3A%2F%2Flogin-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%2Foidc&nonce=equ0MBTPKf0h82CBRborxJ-OMzVTwl-UTQPZ2TuGe4s)> ).
Could you please help me resolve this issue?
Thank you for your support.
Best regards,
Karthika
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / Web Portal Login (Dirac)
Technical Action Required: No (credential workflow guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User unable to log into web portal despite SAFE access. Correct flow: use SAFE-provided password and web TOTP (`CSD3:<userid>`). If login flow appears inconsistent, refresh/reset SAFE password and retry.

ACTION
1) Confirm user ID and expected credential pair (SAFE password + web token label).
2) If not prompted correctly or repeated failure, request password reset through SAFE.
3) Confirm successful login after reset.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Karthika,

Please use the SAFE-provided password together with the web token labelled `CSD3:dc-bhuv1`.

If this still fails, please request a new password through SAFE and retry.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Distinguish web TOTP label from SSH token label.

user_input:
Dear HPC Support team,
could I receive advice on using checkpoints when running a slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
I have found the following documentation useful:[https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html)
sbatch
Several options have been added to support checkpoint restart:
- --checkpoint: Specifies the interval between periodic checkpoint of a batch job. By default, the job will have no checkpoints created. Acceptable time formats include "minutes", "minutes:seconds", "hours:minutes:seconds", "days-hours", "days-hours:minutes" and "days-hours:minutes:seconds".
- --checkpoint-dir:Specify the directory when the checkpoint image files of a batch job will be stored. The default value is the current working directory. Checkpoint files will be of the form "<job_id>.ckpt" for jobs and "<job_id>.<step_id>.ckpt" for job steps.
Environment variables are available for all of these options:
SLURM_CHECKPOINT is equivalent to --checkpoint:
- SLURM_CHECKPOINT_DIR is equivalent to --checkpoint-dir
however, the --checkpoint command does not work from a script of from terminal. According to the slurm-rest-23.02 update log (which I believe is the current version of slurm on HPC):
"* The checkpoint plugin interface and all associated API calls have been removed."
Does this mean it is not possible to use checkpoints on HPC?
Thank you for your help,
Lisa
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Resilience / Checkpointing Policy
Technical Action Required: No (capability clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asked about Slurm native `--checkpoint` functionality. Platform does not provide native Slurm checkpoint plugin support; users must implement checkpointing at application/workflow level. `dmtcp` module exists but compatibility with AlphaFold is user-dependent.

ACTION
1) Clarify no native Slurm checkpoint plugin on this cluster.
2) Recommend user-level checkpoint/restart implementation.
3) Mention `dmtcp` as optional tool to evaluate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Lisa,

Native Slurm checkpoint/restart is not enabled on this cluster, so `--checkpoint` options are not available here.

Checkpointing must be implemented at application/workflow level. We do provide a `dmtcp` module, though compatibility with AlphaFold would need to be tested in your workflow.

Kind regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply Slurm-native checkpoint flags are supported when plugin is absent.
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Checkpointing / Job Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to use checkpointing with SLURM for a long-running job, specifically AlphaFold 2.3.2, but is experiencing issues due to the removal of the checkpoint plugin interface in the current version of SLURM [1]. The user has found documentation on using checkpoints with SLURM, but the --checkpoint command does not work from a script or terminal [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
The user should consider using DMTCP, a checkpoint/restart system that has SLURM support, as an alternative to the removed checkpoint plugin interface [2]. However, the user's application may need to be modified to work with DMTCP. If the user is unable to implement DMTCP, they may need to consider purchasing SL2 hours to access the long queue, which allows for longer job run times [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more information about your application and how it is currently implemented?
- Have you tried using DMTCP with your application, and if so, what issues have you encountered?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your inquiry about checkpointing with SLURM. Unfortunately, the checkpoint plugin interface has been removed in the current version of SLURM. However, you may be able to use DMTCP as an alternative. If you have any further questions or need help implementing DMTCP, please don't hesitate to ask.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The long queue is only available to paying users, and access is limited to those who have purchased SL2 hours [3].
- The use of DMTCP may require modifications to the user's application, and support for this may be limited [2].

Dear HPC support team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's group. I am currently trying to install a Singularity container to run AlphaFold3 on the HPC by using the attached Slurm script which I received from a recent CSD3-AI/ML clinic I attended. I am running into a problem (described below) and would be grateful for your help with trying to fix this.
Issue:
The script seems to have run successfully to install the container to /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3. However, when I navigate to this directory and try to run the container by calling "./run_alphafold3" I get the following error message:
FATAL: permission denied
I was wondering whether you have a sense of why this might be happening and what I would need to do to get this working without the error.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Containers / AlphaFold / Singularity
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The AlphaFold3 container appears to have been created, but the user gets `FATAL: permission denied` when running `./run_alphafold3`. From the visible query alone, the safest interpretation is that the generated launcher script, its execute permissions, or the way it invokes the container needs checking. The next step is to inspect the wrapper script, confirm it is executable, and verify the image/data paths being used.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to show the generated launcher script and its permissions, e.g.:
   - `ls -l run_alphafold3`
   - `head -n 40 run_alphafold3`
2) Confirm the container image exists in the install directory and is readable.
3) If the launcher is not executable, instruct the user to run `chmod +x run_alphafold3`.
4) Review whether the launcher is invoking Singularity correctly for the local installation.
5) Confirm the required AlphaFold data/model weights are available and mapped as documented.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

The `permission denied` error suggests the generated launcher script or the way it is being invoked needs checking.

Please send us:
- the output of `ls -l run_alphafold3`
- the first part of the script (`head -n 40 run_alphafold3`)
- confirmation that the AlphaFold image file exists in the install directory

If the launcher is not executable, please also try:
`chmod +x run_alphafold3`

Once we can see the wrapper contents and permissions, we can confirm the correct Singularity invocation.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume the container image is broken if the wrapper script was generated incorrectly.
- For AlphaFold3, confirm that model weights access has been approved separately.

To resolve this issue, the user should first check the permissions of the `run_alphafold3` script and the directory where the container is installed [1]. If the issue persists, the user may need to re-install the container using the provided SLURM script [2].

```
# Generate run macro run_alphafold3
cat <<EOF> run_alphafold3
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=$INSTALL_DIR/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif 
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \\
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \\
  -B \$DATA:/data \\
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \\
  --nv \${SIMAGE} \\
  python run_alphafold.py \\
  --db_dir=/data
  "\$@"
EOF

chmod +x run_alphafold3
# Use as is: ./run_alphafold3
# with only alphafold3 option flags not described in the script body above.
```
This should create `run_alphafold3` which should work with the gpus.

Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope this email finds you well. I was trying to use BerkeleyGW on exciton calculations. It was very difficult because I have no experience with compilation BerkeleyGW on nvidia nodes before. Therefore, I am seeking your expertise on compilation BerkeleyGW on CSD3.
Thanks a lot!
Best wishes,
Bingcan

Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

2. Use the provided arch.mk file [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the error messages you are seeing during compilation?
- Have you tried loading the necessary modules and using the provided arch.mk file as suggested in the previous ticket [2]?

user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I would like to use the interactive Rstudio (via [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk<https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)>), but I need a newer R version. The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: R Version Management [1][2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting to use a newer version of R (4.5.1) in the interactive RStudio session, which currently loads R version 4.3.1 [1]. The available R modules include R/4.2.0 [1] and R/4.2.2 [2], but it is unclear if R/4.5.1 is available.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To determine if R/4.5.1 is available, check the available R modules by running the command "module avail" and searching for R versions [1][2]. If R/4.5.1 is not available, consider installing a newer version of R or using an alternative version of R that is available [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the output of the command "module avail" for R versions?
- Are there any specific packages or libraries that require R version 4.5.1?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To use a newer version of R in the interactive RStudio session, please try loading a different R module using the command "module load R/<version>" [1]. If you are unsure about the available R versions, you can check by running "module avail" [1]. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when installing new software or modules to avoid conflicts with existing versions [1][3].
- Always verify the availability and compatibility of R versions before attempting to use them [1][2].

user_input:
Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.
[https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9](https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9)
The problem is that I do not have the sudo authentication.
Could you please help me install this package?
Thank you 
Best wishes,
Jiangnan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Platform Incident / Filesystem Performance
Technical Action Required: Yes (investigate possible service-side performance issue)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user’s slow Python setup and `import torch` freezes could reflect either a problem in the user environment or a broader shared-filesystem or service issue. Because the slowdown affects multiple environment-creation methods and both home and `hpc-work`, the first response should check for any active service-side problem before focusing only on the user’s setup.

ACTION
1) Check whether there is a broader service issue affecting Python environment creation or shared filesystems.
2) Ask the user to confirm the login node and working path they are using.
3) If no active incident is found, collect the exact commands and timings needed to separate user-environment issues from platform performance issues.
4) Ask the user to retry once those checks have been completed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stelios,

The symptoms you describe may indicate a broader platform performance issue rather than only a mistake in your Python environment setup.

We are checking this on our side. In the meantime, please confirm which login node and working path you are using so that we can investigate further if needed.

Best regards,
HPC Support

user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Performance / Filesystem
Technical Action Required: Possibly
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue reported is related to slow import times for Python libraries on the HPC nodes, particularly when importing modules from a virtual environment located in the user's RDS hpc-work folder [1][2][3]. Similar issues have been reported in the past and were sometimes resolved by moving the virtual environment to a different location [5] or by using a different package manager [3]. However, the root cause of the issue is not clear and may be related to the Lustre filesystem or other system-wide factors [4].
